evidence_quotes.quote: Video games developed by most "AAA" studios are not complete products anymore, they are a skeleton of a product with a shop attached to them
no matter the amount of influencers the studio paid to pretend like the game was good.

paragraphs.text: Hello and thank you very much for what you are doing! I have pretty much stopped buying video games, even though that is by far my favorite form of entertainment, specifically because of the consumer abuse that the video game industry has standardised over the last couple of decades. This manifests itself through so many different ways that it is hard to list all of them but here is a small summary of what I think is the core issue: Video games developed by most "AAA" studios are not complete products anymore, they are a skeleton of a product with a shop attached to them, only if the game becomes the "new big thing" and enough kids get addicted to taking their parents credit cards to purchase these never ending "microtransactions" then the game actually continues being developed into what might end up, after a few years, becoming a fully fledged product. This very rarely happens though, in fact, as far as I can see, this practically never happens. Every studio tries to make a new "Fortnite" or "Overwatch", but because most of the big studios are pretty much doing the same thing, the market is over saturated and the vast majority of projects fail. When a game is deemed to have failed, it is simply shut down. Thanks to most single player games now seeing online functionality being shoehorned into them, this means that most recent video games either end up closing down shortly after release, or will be shut down as soon as the studio decides it wants to move on to something else. Consumers simply do not own their products anymore. I recently worked for a US studio that created one of these failures. No one I know of working in the studio (outside of the directors who were contractually obligated to pretend) ever thought the game stood a chance, it was bland, clearly made to copy Fortnite with the least possible effort and try to get kids hooked into buying skins, there was literally nothing else to the game. This was so egregious that the project failed before even releasing. Hundreds of millions of dollars went into the supposed development of this game, less than 20 people played it's beta release, it was a laughable failure that every single person working in the studio knew was going to happen, long in advance, no matter the amount of influencers the studio paid to pretend like the game was good. I do not think that anyone involved in the decision making even believed they were really developing a video game. Video games may not sound important, but they are a multi billion dollar industry nowadays, and a large portion of that money is made off of making kids addicted to buying skins. Working there felt like I was dealing drugs on a street corner next to a school. There was no interest in trying to push the technology, or even design anything original (what the industry pretends to be doing in their marketing), the only requirement for employment there is that you go along and smile with what the director is saying, it was the most surreal experience of my life. Any movement towards anything positive on this front would be an extremely welcome development and I wish you all the best in trying to reduce these dark patterns and other exploitative measures, that are mostly aimed at children and people with addiction issues.
